1	\chapter{Relative Picard sheaf --- \texttt{Scheme.Modules.tensorObj} substrate (A.1.c.SubT)}
2	\label{chap:Picard_TensorObjSubstrate}
3	
4	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
5	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
6	
7	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
8	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
9	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
10	\[
11	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
12	  \qquad
13	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
14	\]
15	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
16	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
17	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
18	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
19	
20	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
21	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
22	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
23	% groups)
24	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
25	% SOURCE QUOTE:
26	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
27	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
28	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
29	% abelian groups defined by the formula
30	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
31	% \end{dfn}
32	%  [\dots]
33	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
34	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
35	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
36	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
37	% topologies  by
38	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
39	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
40	%  \end{dfn}"
41	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
42	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
43	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
44	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
45	homomorphisms.}
46	
47	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
48	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
49	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
50	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
51	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
52	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
53	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
54	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
55	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
56	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
57	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
58	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
59	structure. This is the content of
60	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
61	
62	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
63	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
64	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
65	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
66	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
67	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
68	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
69	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
70	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
71	requires on the Lean carrier is therefore only:
72	\begin{enumerate}
73	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
74	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
75	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
76	    isomorphism classes;
77	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
78	    for \(\otimes\);
79	  \item four existence-of-isomorphism facts on \emph{line bundles}
80	    (locally-trivial rank-one modules): associativity
81	    \((L \otimes M) \otimes N \cong L \otimes (M \otimes N)\), unit
82	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes L \cong L\), commutativity
83	    \(L \otimes M \cong M \otimes L\), and inverse
84	    \(L \otimes L^{-1} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\) with
85	    \(L^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\).
86	\end{enumerate}
87	At Mathlib's pinned commit, only a presheaf-level version of (1) is
88	available; \(\mathcal{O}_X\) and the dual are present as scheme-level
89	objects. The four facts of (3) all reduce to a single non-trivial
90	ingredient --- the \emph{global} compatibility isomorphism
91	\(\mathtt{tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\) (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso})
92	expressing that \(\otimes_X\) commutes with restriction to an open. Local
93	triviality alone does \emph{not} furnish a global associator: two sheaves
94	each locally isomorphic to \(\mathcal{O}\) need not be globally isomorphic
95	(\(\mathcal{O}(1)\) versus \(\mathcal{O}\) on \(\mathbb{P}^1\)). The global
96	compatibility isomorphism is nonetheless available for \emph{arbitrary}
97	modules \(M, N\), because the relevant restriction is along an \emph{open
98	immersion}: restriction is then computed sectionwise, and the comparison of
99	structure-sheaf rings over the image is the \emph{isomorphism}
100	\(f.\mathtt{appIso}\), so the base change is extension of scalars along a ring
101	isomorphism --- which is automatically an isomorphism and commutes with
102	\(\otimes\). No line-bundle or flatness hypothesis is needed. This is
103	exactly the carrier on which the relative Picard group law of
104	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} rests, and it is the subject of
105	\cref{sec:tensorobj_substrate,sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift} below. The full symmetric monoidal category on
106	\emph{all} quasi-coherent modules is \emph{not} required; see
107	\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
108	
109	\section{Mathlib API survey}
110	\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}
111	
112	The relevant pieces of the Mathlib API at the pinned commit
113	(\texttt{b80f227}) split across two layers.
114	
115	\paragraph{Layer 1: presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure.} Mathlib's
116	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules} module
117	constructs a monoidal-category structure on the category
118	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves of modules over a presheaf of
119	commutative rings \(R\); in particular, it exposes a tensor-product
120	operation \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) realising the
121	sectionwise tensor product
122	\[
123	  (M \otimes_R N)(U) \;\;=\;\; M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)
124	\]
125	for open sets / objects \(U\) in the indexing site, together with the
126	associator, left/right unitors, and braiding making the resulting
127	category symmetric monoidal. This is the presheaf-level analogue of
128	Stacks tag 03DM (relative tensor product of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules).
129	
130	\paragraph{Layer 2: scheme-level \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.} The module
131	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) on a scheme \(X\) (the Lean target
132	expressed by the namespace \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules})
133	realises the category of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
134	on the small Zariski site of \(X\). It comes with pullback functoriality
135	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\), used heavily in
136	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}), pullback comparison isomorphisms
137	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp}\),
138	\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr}\)), and the structure-sheaf
139	constant module \(\mathcal{O}_X\). It does \emph{not} ship a binary
140	tensor-product operation
141	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \to
142	\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance.
143	
144	\paragraph{The gap.} The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)
145	this chapter introduces is the named operation lifting
146	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) from Layer 1 into
147	Layer 2, packaged so that the consumer at chapter
148	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} can read it as a binary operation on
149	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\). The \emph{single} genuinely
150	non-trivial fact about this operation that the group law needs is the
151	\emph{restriction-compatibility isomorphism}
152	\(\mathtt{tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\) (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}):
153	for \emph{arbitrary} modules \(M, N\) and the restriction along an open immersion
154	\(f\), the canonical comparison
155	\((M \otimes_X N)|_f \cong M|_f \otimes N|_f\) is an isomorphism. This is
156	the \emph{only} input the four existence-of-iso group-law lemmas of
157	\cref{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift} consume. Because \(f\) is an open immersion,
158	restriction is sectionwise and the structure-sheaf comparison is the ring
159	isomorphism \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\); the comparison is then base change along that
160	ring isomorphism, an isomorphism commuting with \(\otimes\) by
161	\(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalarsEquivalenceOfRingEquiv}\). The sole
162	project-side ingredient is a bounded sectionwise unfolding of
163	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\) along the open immersion; no
164	flatness and no line-bundle hypothesis enter (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}).
165	The project does \emph{not} require a global \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance
166	on all quasi-coherent \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a
167	\(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) structure: monoidal coherence is never consumed
168	by a group law on iso-classes (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
169	The project's policy on this gap is \emph{project-side}: the substrate is
170	built inside \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (the file
171	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\) is its home)
172	and the consumer instance is closed against it. A parallel Mathlib upstream
173	PR with the same content is welcome but not on the critical path for the
174	project; the consumer is closed against the project-side substrate
175	independently of any upstream timing.
176	
177	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
178	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
179	
180	\begin{definition}
181	
182	\leanok
183	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
184	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
185	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
186	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
187	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
188	  \[
189	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
190	  \]
191	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
192	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
193	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
194	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
195	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
196	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
197	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
198	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
199	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
200	  canonical compatibility
201	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
202	     \;\cong\;
203	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
204	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
205	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
206	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
207	
208	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
209	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
210	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
211	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
212	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
213	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
214	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
215	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
216	\end{definition}
217	
218	\begin{lemma}
219	
220	\leanok
221	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
222	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
223	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
224	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
225	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
226	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
227	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
228	  \[
229	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
230	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
231	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
232	  \]
233	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
234	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
235	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
236	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
237	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
238	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
239	  \[
240	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
241	    \qquad
242	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
243	  \]
244	  the associator
245	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
246	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
247	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
248	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they have no current Lean
249	  declaration and are off the critical path, recorded in
250	  \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
251	\end{lemma}
252	
253	\begin{proof}
254	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
255	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
256	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
257	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
258	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
259	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
260	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
261	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
262	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
263	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
264	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
265	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
266	\end{proof}
267	
268	\begin{remark}
269	[The full monoidal category on all modules is off the critical path]
270	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
271	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
272	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
273	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
274	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
275	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
276	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
277	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This chapter deliberately does
278	  \emph{not} pursue that, and the relative Picard group law does \emph{not}
279	  depend on it.
280	
281	  The reason is that the relative Picard group law of
282	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on \emph{isomorphism
283	  classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
284	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
285	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
286	  coherence data of a monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
287	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never consumed by the group
288	  law. Conversely, building the global \(\MonoidalCategory\) on all
289	  quasi-coherent modules would route through the localization-monoidal
290	  transport \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal}\), which demands a
291	  \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\,W\) instance for the
292	  sheafification-localizing morphism property \(W\); the
293	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) stability \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\)
294	  of that property, for an \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\), is provable only
295	  through a \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) ---
296	  absent from Mathlib at the pinned commit, and a multi-file build. That cost
297	  buys coherence the group law never uses.
298	
299	  The group law needs only the four existence-of-iso lemmas of
300	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}, each assembled from the
301	  restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}. We
302	  therefore record the global monoidal category as explicitly \emph{off the
303	  critical path} and build the group law directly on the line-bundle
304	  subcategory. This mirrors Mathlib's own Picard group
305	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}),
306	  whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is transported from
307	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\) over
308	  a fixed ring and needs no sheafification, no localization, and no
309	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\).
310	\end{remark}
311	
312	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
313	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
314	
315	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
316	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
317	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
318	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
319	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section first isolates the one
320	genuinely-hard ingredient --- the restriction-compatibility
321	isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} --- and then records the
322	\emph{four existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity,
323	unit, commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law
324	consumes, each assembled from that ingredient on a common trivialising
325	cover, together with their restriction to the
326	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype and the interaction with the
327	pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). These four facts (\emph{not} a
328	\(\MonoidalCategory\) instance) are exactly the group-law data; cf.\
329	\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
330	
331	\begin{lemma}
332	[Sectionwise lax-monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\)]
333	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
334	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal}
335	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
336	  % NOTE: this CommRingCat-level lax-monoidal instance
337	  % (`PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal`, with helpers
338	  % `restrictScalarsLaxε`, `restrictScalarsLaxμ`) is a self-contained
339	  % supplement. It is NOT used by `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso`, whose proof
340	  % (open-immersion sectionwise base change) identifies restriction along an
341	  % open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
342	  % needs no lax-monoidal pushforward. Retained for potential reuse; off the
343	  % critical path for the tensor group law.
344	  Let \(\varphi : R \to S\) be a morphism of presheaves of \emph{commutative}
345	  rings on a site (so that both \(R\) and \(S\) factor through
346	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and the category of presheaves of modules over each
347	  is monoidal). Then the presheaf-of-modules restriction-of-scalars functor
348	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) is lax monoidal.
349	  Consequently, composing with the already-monoidal Mathlib functor
350	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) via
351	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\), the full pushforward
352	  \[
353	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi
354	      \;\;:=\;\;
355	    \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \;\circ\; \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi
356	  \]
357	  is lax monoidal. This lemma is a self-contained
358	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-level lax-monoidal supplement: it stands on its own
359	  and is retained for potential reuse, but it is \emph{off the critical path}
360	  for the tensor group law. In particular it is \emph{not} an ingredient of
361	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, whose proof identifies restriction along an
362	  open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
363	  consequently needs no lax-monoidal structure on the pushforward.
364	\end{lemma}
365	
366	\begin{proof}
367	  This is a \emph{sectionwise} lift of the existing fact that, for a ring map
368	  \(f : R \to S\), the module restriction-of-scalars functor
369	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,f\) is lax monoidal
370	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction}, where it is
371	  obtained as the \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\) of the
372	  extension/restriction adjunction \(\mathtt{extendRestrictScalarsAdj}\) ---
373	  i.e.\ Mathlib already uses the same mate idiom one level down). The presheaf
374	  monoidal structure is computed sectionwise,
375	  \((M_1 \otimes M_2).\mathrm{obj}\,X = M_1.\mathrm{obj}\,X \otimes M_2.\mathrm{obj}\,X\),
376	  and the presheaf functor \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) acts
377	  sectionwise as \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,X)\).
378	  Hence the lax structure maps \(\mu\) (laxity) and \(\varepsilon\) (unit)
379	  assemble from the per-section \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\) ones, the required
380	  naturality squares being the naturality of the sectionwise maps against the
381	  presheaf restriction morphisms. Packaging these as a
382	  \(\mathtt{Functor.CoreLaxMonoidal}\) yields the lax-monoidal structure; no
383	  new diagram chase is needed beyond transporting the sectionwise coherence.
384	  The composite \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) is then lax monoidal by
385	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\) together with the monoidality of
386	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) (Mathlib), modulo a defeq
387	  transport across the definitional unfolding of \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\).
388	
389	  The commutativity hypothesis is essential: the presheaf-of-modules monoidal
390	  category requires \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued presheaves of rings, so the
391	  instance must be stated over \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-factored \(R, S\). In
392	  the project this holds: the relevant \(\varphi\) is
393	  \((\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\), which is
394	  structure-sheaf-valued and hence commutative-ring-valued.
395	\end{proof}
396	
397	\begin{lemma}
398	
399	\leanok
400	[Tensor product commutes with restriction along an open immersion]
401	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
402	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_restrict_iso}
403	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
404	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be
405	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, and let
406	  \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion, with \((-)|_f\) the
407	  restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along \(f\). The canonical comparison
408	  morphism
409	  \[
410	    (M \otimes_X N)\big|_f
411	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
412	    M\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; N\big|_f
413	  \]
414	  is an isomorphism. No local-freeness, flatness, or line-bundle hypothesis on
415	  \(M, N\) is required: the statement holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
416	  The reason is structural to open immersions --- restriction along \(f\) is
417	  base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{isomorphism} induced by \(f\),
418	  not along a general ring map --- and base change along a ring isomorphism is
419	  trivially invertible and commutes with tensor products. The intended Lean
420	  target is
421	  \(\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\).
422	\end{lemma}
423	
424	\begin{proof}
425	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
426	  Write \(\varphi\) for the morphism of structure-sheaf-valued presheaves of
427	  rings induced by the open immersion \(f\) (concretely
428	  \(\varphi = (\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\)), so that
429	  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor on
430	  presheaves of modules. The proof exploits the defining feature of an open
431	  immersion: the comparison of structure-sheaf rings over the image is an
432	  \emph{isomorphism}, not merely a ring map. For an open \(V \subseteq U\) the
433	  section ring \(\Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr)\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) over the image
434	  of \(V\) is identified with \(\Gamma(U, V)\) by the canonical \emph{ring
435	  isomorphism}
436	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V : \Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\sim}
437	  \Gamma(U, V)\)
438	  (the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) isomorphism
439	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.appIso}\)). Restriction along \(f\) is
440	  therefore extension of scalars along
441	  this ring isomorphism, and the proof needs no flatness anywhere.
442	
443	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The restriction
444	  \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion \(f\) is
445	  naturally isomorphic to the abstract presheaf-of-modules pullback
446	  \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\), via the
447	  present comparison \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). It
448	  therefore suffices to produce the comparison and prove it an isomorphism for
449	  the abstract pullback functor.
450	
451	  \emph{Step 2 (move the pullback inside the sheafification).} The substrate
452	  \(\otimes_X\) is sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
453	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}). The pullback commutes with
454	  sheafification: there is a natural isomorphism
455	  \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
456	   \;\cong\;
457	   \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\)
458	  (the present \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\)). Hence the
459	  whole question descends to the presheaf level, where it is the base-change
460	  comparison
461	  \[
462	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(A \otimes B)
463	      \;\longrightarrow\;
464	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
465	      \;\otimes\;
466	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B
467	  \]
468	  for the underlying presheaves of modules \(A, B\) of \(M\) and \(N\).
469	
470	  \emph{Step 3 (sectionwise identification along the open-immersion ring
471	  isomorphism).} The functor \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\) is
472	  \emph{defined} as the left adjoint of pushforward and is consequently
473	  \emph{opaque} on objects and morphisms: Mathlib provides no sectionwise
474	  formula for it, and --- unlike the scheme-level restriction --- no
475	  presheaf-level analogue of \(\mathtt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). The one
476	  genuinely-new project-side ingredient of this proof is therefore a
477	  \emph{sectionwise unfolding} of the abstract pullback along the open
478	  immersion: for the open-immersion ring morphism \(\varphi\), the presheaf
479	  \((\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,P\) is, section by section over an
480	  open \(V\), the base change of the sections of \(P\) along the ring
481	  \emph{isomorphism} \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V\) --- that is, restriction of
482	  sections combined with scalar transport across the isomorphism. (Over an open
483	  immersion the sections themselves agree definitionally,
484	  \(\Gamma(M|_f, V) = \Gamma(M, f(V))\) by the \(\mathtt{restrict\_obj}\)
485	  identity; only the scalar ring is transported, and it is transported along an
486	  isomorphism.)
487	
488	  Granting this sectionwise description, the base-change comparison of Step 2
489	  is, on each section over \(V\), the canonical map of a tensor product under
490	  base change along the ring isomorphism \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V\). Base change
491	  along a ring isomorphism is a \emph{monoidal equivalence} of module
492	  categories --- it is invertible and carries tensor products to tensor products
493	  --- by \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalarsEquivalenceOfRingEquiv}\) applied to
494	  the ring equivalence underlying \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V\). Hence each
495	  sectionwise comparison is an isomorphism compatible with \(\otimes\). No
496	  flatness, no \(\mathtt{extendScalars}\)-monoidality, and no strong-monoidal
497	  pullback instance is invoked: the entire force of the step is that the scalar
498	  change is along an \emph{isomorphism}.
499	
500	  \emph{Gluing.} The sectionwise isomorphisms of Step 3 are natural in \(V\)
501	  (compatible with the presheaf restriction maps, the naturality square being
502	  that of \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\) against restriction). They therefore assemble,
503	  via \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.isoMk}\), into a single presheaf-of-modules
504	  isomorphism realising the base-change comparison of Step 2 as an iso.
505	  Transporting this back through the sheafification units of Step 2 and the
506	  restriction--pullback comparison of Step 1 yields the asserted global
507	  isomorphism \((M \otimes_X N)|_f \cong M|_f \otimes_U N|_f\).
508	
509	  The route uses no monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\) or on the

529	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}

532	  rank one (i.e.\ line bundles). The tensor product \(L \otimes_X L'\) of

540	  Pick a common affine open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) on which both
541	  \(L|_{U_i}\) and \(L'|_{U_i}\) are free of rank one. On each \(U_i\)
542	  there are trivialisations \(L|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) and
543	  \(L'|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). The tensor product of two free
544	  rank-one modules over a commutative ring is again free of rank one;
545	  hence \((L \otimes_X L')|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). So
546	  \(L \otimes_X L'\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an object of

769	\section{Consumer: the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance for the relative Picard sheaf}
770	\label{sec:tensorobj_consumer_acg}
771	
772	The substrate of \cref{sec:tensorobj_substrate,sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
773	supplies all the data missing from \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}'s Lean
774	encoding. The consumer instance below packages the abelian-group law
775	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
776	 := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\) on the
777	relative Picard quotient via the substrate.
778	
779	\begin{theorem}
780	
781	\leanok
782	[Abelian-group instance on the relative Picard quotient via \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)]
783	  \label{thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj}
784	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup_via_tensorObj}
785	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
786	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
787	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
788	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
789	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso,
790	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible,
791	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
792	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
793	
794	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
795	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
796	  % abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
797	  % groups)
798	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
799	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
800	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
801	  %  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
802	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
803	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
804	  % 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
805	  % \end{dfn}
806	  %  [\dots]
807	  % \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
808	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
809	  % 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
810	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
811	  % topologies  by
812	  % 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
813	  % 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
814	  %  \end{dfn}"
815	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
816	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
817	  groups; the relative Picard functor is a quotient of abelian groups).}
818	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
819	  field \(k\), let \(\pi_C : C \to \Spec k\) be the structure morphism,
820	  and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme with structure morphism
821	  \(\pi_T : T \to \Spec k\). The set
822	  \[
823	    \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
824	  \]
825	  of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries a canonical
826	  abelian-group structure with addition
827	  \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
828	   := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_k T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
829	  neutral element \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\,\bigr]\), and
830	  inverse
831	  \(-\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\),
832	  where \(\otimes_{C \times_k T}\) and \((-)^{-1}\) are the operations
833	  supplied by the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
834	  restricted to the \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carrier
835	  via \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}. With this structure the quotient
836	  map
837	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is a
838	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\),
839	  in agreement with \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
840	\end{theorem}
841	
842	\begin{proof}
843	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
844	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
845	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
846	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
847	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso,
848	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible,
849	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
850	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
851	  Write \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the set of isomorphism classes of objects
852	  of \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) (line bundles on
853	  \(C \times_k T\)), with the operation
854	  \([L] + [L'] := [L \otimes_{C \times_k T} L']\). This operation is
855	  well-defined on isomorphism classes: an isomorphism \(L \cong L_0\) tensors
856	  to an isomorphism \(L \otimes L' \cong L_0 \otimes L'\) by functoriality of
857	  \(\otimes\) (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}), and
858	  symmetrically in the second argument. The group axioms are then
859	  \emph{exactly} the four existence-of-isomorphism facts established above,
860	  read on isomorphism classes:
861	  \begin{itemize}
862	    \item \textbf{associativity} \(([L]+[M])+[N] = [L]+([M]+[N])\) is
863	      \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso};
864	    \item \textbf{neutral element} \([\mathcal{O}_{C\times_k T}] + [L] = [L]\)
865	      (and on the right) is \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso};
866	    \item \textbf{commutativity} \([L]+[M]=[M]+[L]\) is
867	      \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso};
868	    \item \textbf{inverse} \([L] + [L^{-1}] = [\mathcal{O}_{C\times_k T}]\),
869	      with \(L^{-1}=\mathcal{H}om(L,\mathcal{O}_{C\times_k T})\), is
870	      \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}.
871	  \end{itemize}
872	  Each axiom is a \emph{proposition} --- the existence of an isomorphism ---
873	  so no monoidal-coherence datum (pentagon, triangle, hexagon) is invoked or
874	  required; the \(\AddCommGroup\) on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is assembled
875	  directly from these four \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\)
876	  statements. This mirrors Mathlib's own Picard group:
877	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) carries its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\)
878	  (\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\), \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup})
879	  transported from
880	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\), the
881	  iso-class arithmetic being the family of existence-of-iso facts
882	  \([M \otimes N] = [M]\cdot[N]\) and \([M^{\vee}] = [M]^{-1}\); here the same
883	  shape is transported to the scheme-level line-bundle subcategory
884	  \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\). (The scheme-level Picard
885	  group is genuinely absent from Mathlib --- \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}
886	  records ``connect to invertible sheaves'' as an open task --- so the project
887	  supplies it lightly.) The invertibility-predicate carrier is the project's
888	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\), mirroring Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\)
889	  verbatim.
890	
891	  By \cref{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}, the pullback functor
892	  \(\pi_T^{*} : \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \to
893	   \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carries tensor products to
894	  tensor products and the unit to the unit, so the induced map on isomorphism
895	  classes \(\pi_T^{*} : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a group
896	  homomorphism, and its image is a subgroup.
897	
898	  By \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}, the underlying set of
899	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of cosets of this subgroup. The
900	  quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup is itself an abelian group, via
901	  the standard \(\mathtt{QuotientAddGroup}\) construction, and the quotient
902	  map is a surjective group homomorphism whose kernel is exactly the subgroup
903	  (Stacks tag 01CR for the Picard-group special case; the general
904	  quotient-of-abelian-groups statement is in any algebra textbook). Putting
905	  the pieces together gives the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance on
906	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) of the statement, and identifies it with the
907	  structure of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
908	\end{proof}

922	The Lean target file is the new
